name: Apsara
description: Apsaras are celestial nymphs in Hindu mythology, known for their beauty, youth, and dancing skills. They are often associated with water, clouds, and fertility. Apsaras are youthful and elegant, possessing supernatural powers and a captivating allure. They are skilled dancers, musicians, and entertainers, residing in Indra's court (Svarga) or in the celestial realms. Apsaras are sent to earth by the gods, especially Indra, to seduce sages or break their asceticism if their power becomes too great. They are often depicted as beautiful women adorned with exquisite jewelry and clothing, gracefully performing dances that enchant both gods and mortals. They are not immortal but have a long lifespan. They are not inherently good or evil but act according to the will of the gods. Some are born from the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana), while others are created at will by deities.
subtitle: Celestial nymphs of beauty and dance
appearance: Apsaras are depicted as extraordinarily beautiful and youthful women with slender figures and graceful movements. They are adorned with elaborate jewelry, including necklaces, earrings, bracelets, and anklets. Their clothing is typically flowing and colorful, often made of silk or other fine fabrics. Their hair is long, dark, and often adorned with flowers or jewels. They have captivating eyes and a radiant smile. They are often depicted carrying musical instruments or performing graceful dance poses.
habitat: Apsaras primarily reside in the celestial realms, particularly Indra's court (Svarga), the abode of the gods. They are also associated with water bodies, such as rivers, lakes, and oceans, as well as clouds and forests. They descend to earth when summoned by the gods or when their presence is required for a specific purpose.
behavior: Apsaras are generally playful, flirtatious, and mischievous. They are skilled entertainers and enjoy dancing, singing, and creating illusions. They are often sent by Indra to test the virtue of sages or to disrupt their meditation. While not inherently malicious, they are obedient to the gods and will carry out their commands, even if it means deceiving or seducing mortals. They are known for their ability to adapt to different situations and to charm those around them.

keyMyths.title: Menaka and Vishwamitra
keyMyths.description: Indra sends the Apsara Menaka to seduce the sage Vishwamitra, whose ascetic power threatens Indra's position. Menaka succeeds, breaking Vishwamitra's meditation and leading to the birth of Shakuntala.

keyMyths.title: Urvashi and Arjuna
keyMyths.description: The Apsara Urvashi is attracted to Arjuna, but he rejects her advances, considering her a mother figure due to her past relationship with his ancestor. Angered, Urvashi curses Arjuna to become impotent for a year.
